---
title: "Convolution and Blur"
subtitle: "MATH 435: Mathematical Imaging - Week 2"
author: "Laurent Najman"
date: "Week 2"
format:
  revealjs:
    incremental: false
execute:
  enabled: false
---

## Opening Question {.inverse-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
From pixels to operators
:::

What does it mean, mathematically, for an image to be blurred?

## Today

::: {.checklist}
- Build convolution from a local weighted average.
- Interpret the point spread function (PSF).
- Apply 2D convolution to real images.
- Understand boundary conditions.
- See why deblurring is already a hard inverse problem.
:::

## 75-Minute Plan

| Time | Work |
|---:|---|
| 0-10 min | review fixed forward models |
| 10-25 min | 1D convolution by hand |
| 25-40 min | 2D convolution and PSF |
| 40-55 min | real image blur demo |
| 55-65 min | boundary conditions |
| 65-72 min | why deblurring is hard |
| 72-75 min | exit question |

## Reminder from Week 1

::: {.model-box}
For a fixed measurement rule, we often write

$$
y = Ax + \eta.
$$

Today, $A$ will be a blur operator.
:::

::: {.caption}
The blur kernel is assumed fixed. That is different from unknown scene occlusion or nonlinear visibility.
:::

## Part 1: 1D Convolution {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
First in one dimension
:::

Convolution as a weighted neighborhood average

## Local Weighted Average

Suppose a signal is noisy or sharp at one point. A simple smoothing rule is:

$$
y_i = \frac{1}{4}x_{i-1} + \frac{1}{2}x_i + \frac{1}{4}x_{i+1}.
$$

::: {.definition-box}
The output at index $i$ is built from a small neighborhood of the input.
:::

## The Kernel

The weights

$$
h =
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{1}{4} & \frac{1}{2} & \frac{1}{4}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

are called a convolution kernel.

::: {.question-box}
What should happen if the weights sum to 1?
:::

## Activity 1: Compute by Hand

::: {.time-tag}
8 minutes
:::

Let

$$
x =
\begin{bmatrix}
0 & 0 & 1 & 3 & 2 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

and $h = [1/4, 1/2, 1/4]$.

Compute the output at the central indices using zero values outside the signal.

## Activity 1 Debrief

For the peak location,

$$
y_4 = \frac{1}{4}x_3 + \frac{1}{2}x_4 + \frac{1}{4}x_5
     = \frac{1}{4}(1) + \frac{1}{2}(3) + \frac{1}{4}(2)
     = 2.25.
$$

::: {.takeaway-box}
The peak is reduced, and its mass is spread to neighboring locations.
:::

## 1D Convolution Picture

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week02-1d-convolution.png){fig-alt="One dimensional signal, convolution kernel, and blurred output"}
:::

## Convolution Formula

One common discrete convention is

$$
(h * x)_i = \sum_{k} h_k x_{i-k}.
$$

::: {.caption}
For symmetric kernels, convolution and correlation give the same result. For nonsymmetric kernels, the sign convention matters.
:::

## Three Useful Properties

For a fixed kernel $h$, convolution is:

::: {.three-col}
::: {.model-box}
::: {.tag}
Linear
:::

$h*(a x + b z) = a(h*x) + b(h*z)$
:::

::: {.definition-box}
::: {.tag}
Local
:::

Each output uses nearby values.
:::

::: {.question-box}
::: {.tag}
Shift-invariant
:::

The same rule is applied everywhere.
:::
:::

## Part 2: 2D Convolution {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
From signals to images
:::

A kernel becomes a small image

## 2D Formula

For an image $x[i,j]$ and a kernel $h[a,b]$:

$$
y[i,j] = (h*x)[i,j]
      = \sum_{a,b} h[a,b] x[i-a,j-b].
$$

::: {.definition-box}
The output pixel is a weighted combination of nearby input pixels.
:::

## Point Spread Function

::: {.model-box}
::: {.tag}
PSF
:::

The point spread function describes how the imaging system records a single ideal bright point.
:::

If one point becomes a small blob, then every point in the image is spread in the same way.

## Example Kernels

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week02-kernels.png){fig-alt="Identity, box average, gaussian, and motion blur kernels"}
:::

## Kernel Interpretation

| Kernel | Visual effect | Typical model |
|---|---|---|
| identity | no blur | perfect sampling |
| box average | blocky smoothing | simple local average |
| gaussian | smooth blur | defocus or optical smoothing |
| motion | directional streak | camera or object motion |

## Fixed Blur Is Linear

If $h$ is fixed, then

$$
y = h * x
$$

is a linear operator on the image.

::: {.caption}
This is a controlled blur model. It does not say all visual image formation is linear.
:::

## Part 3: Blur on a Real Image {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
Now look at pixels
:::

Different kernels, different blur

## Real Image Blur

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week02-real-blur.png){fig-alt="Real grayscale image with box, gaussian, and motion blur"}
:::

## What Changed?

::: {.two-col}
::: {.definition-box}
Blur removes local contrast:

- edges become less sharp;
- fine textures disappear;
- neighboring pixels become more similar.
:::

::: {.question-box}
::: {.tag}
Discussion
:::

Which blur looks easiest to undo? Which looks hardest?
:::
:::

## Python Demo: Blur a Real Image {.code-small}

```{python}
from skimage import data
from scipy import signal
import numpy as np

image = data.camera().astype(float) / 255
kernel = np.ones((13, 13)) / (13 * 13)

blurred = signal.convolve2d(
    image, kernel, mode="same", boundary="symm"
)
```

## Run Today's Code {.code-small}

From the repository root:

```bash
python3 examples/week02_convolution_blur.py
python3 examples/make_week02_figures.py
./scripts/quarto render
```

::: {.caption}
The first command prints numerical outputs. The second regenerates the figures used in this deck.
:::

## Why Normalize the Kernel?

For many blur kernels, we impose

$$
\sum_{a,b} h[a,b] = 1.
$$

::: {.takeaway-box}
This preserves the average brightness of the image.
:::

## Mini-Check

::: {.exercise-box}
::: {.tag}
2 minutes
:::

If a $3 \times 3$ box blur has equal weights, what is each weight?

What happens if the weights sum to 2 instead of 1?
:::

## Part 4: Boundary Conditions {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
The edge of the image
:::

What does the kernel see outside the image?

## Why Boundaries Matter

Near the image boundary, a kernel asks for pixels that do not exist.

For example, at the top-left pixel:

$$
y[1,1] = \sum_{a,b} h[a,b] x[1-a,1-b].
$$

Some indices fall outside the image.

## Boundary Choices

| Mode | Outside value | Possible artifact |
|---|---|---|
| zero padding | black outside | dark borders |
| reflect | mirror image | smoother edges |
| wrap | periodic image | artificial wraparound |
| crop/valid | do not compute boundary | smaller output |

## Boundary Effects

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week02-boundary-effects.png){fig-alt="Boundary modes: original, zero padding, reflect, and wrap"}
:::

## Activity 2: Choose a Boundary Rule

::: {.time-tag}
6 minutes
:::

::: {.exercise-box}
For each application, choose a boundary condition and justify it:

1. a microscope image of cells;
2. a panoramic texture;
3. a medical slice with black background;
4. a small cropped photograph.
:::

## Part 5: Matrix View {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
Back to Week 1
:::

Convolution is a linear operator when the kernel is fixed

## Convolution as a Matrix

For a four-pixel 1D signal, same-size output, zero boundary conditions, and
$h = [h_{-1}, h_0, h_1]$:

$$
y =
\begin{bmatrix}
h_0    & h_1 & 0   & 0 \\
h_{-1} & h_0 & h_1 & 0 \\
0      & h_{-1} & h_0 & h_1 \\
0      & 0   & h_{-1} & h_0
\end{bmatrix}
x.
$$

## What the Matrix Shows

::: {.takeaway-box}
The blur matrix has repeated diagonals because the same local rule is applied at each position.
:::

::: {.question-box}
What changes in this matrix if we use periodic boundary conditions?
:::

## Part 6: Why Deblurring Is Hard {.section-slide}

::: {.section-kicker}
The inverse problem appears
:::

If blur is linear, why not just invert it?

## The Naive Deblurring Idea

If

$$
y = Ax + \eta,
$$

then the tempting answer is

$$
x = A^{-1}y.
$$

::: {.question-box}
What could go wrong here?
:::

## First Look at Instability

::: {.figure-frame}
![](../assets/figures/week02-deblur-instability.png){fig-alt="Clean signal, blurred signal, noisy blurred signal, and unstable naive inverse"}
:::

## Why It Fails

Deblurring can fail because:

1. blur removes high-frequency detail;
2. small singular values make inversion unstable;
3. noise is amplified by inverse filtering;
4. the blur kernel may be unknown or only approximately known.

::: {.caption}
Fourier analysis will make this precise in the next lectures.
:::

## Modeling Caveat

::: {.definition-box}
Some blur is well modeled as fixed convolution.
:::

::: {.question-box}
Other image degradations are not simple convolution:

- depth-dependent defocus;
- nonlinear camera saturation;
- occlusion;
- spatially varying motion.
:::

## In-Class Modeling Question

::: {.exercise-box}
::: {.tag}
Discuss
:::

You receive a blurry photo from a phone camera.

What evidence would make you believe the blur is approximately convolutional?

What evidence would make you reject the convolution model?
:::

## End-of-Class Checkpoint

::: {.exercise-box}
For each item, give one sentence:

1. What is a PSF?
2. Why should a blur kernel often sum to 1?
3. Name two boundary conditions.
4. Why is deblurring sensitive to noise?
:::

## Suggested Answers

| Question | Short answer |
|---|---|
| PSF | image of one ideal point after the imaging system |
| kernel sum | preserves average brightness |
| boundary | zero, reflect, wrap, valid/crop |
| noise | inverse filtering amplifies weak frequencies |

## What Students Should Remember

::: {.takeaway-box}
- Convolution is a local weighted average.
- The PSF is the blur kernel of an imaging system.
- Boundary conditions are part of the model.
- Fixed convolution is linear, but not all image formation is convolution.
- Deblurring is an inverse problem and is usually unstable.
:::

## After Class

::: {.checklist}
- Use the [class roadmap](../classes.html) to find the book chapter, notebook, and weekly practice prompt.
- Run the week notebook and change at least one important parameter.
- Write one claim-evidence-limit sentence about today's model.
:::

## Next Time

Fourier transform in imaging:

- frequency interpretation of blur;
- convolution theorem;
- low-pass and high-pass behavior;
- why inverse filtering amplifies noise.
